attacking_style: Launches late-climb attacks in final 4-8km, often after GC groups coalesce. Prefers technical finales where positioning and timing trump raw power. Shows willingness to attack from chase groups when GC pressure is off.
team_tactics: Often isolated or reliant on minimal support; team struggles with positioning and echelon control. When GC pressure relaxed, allowed individual stage-hunting freedom. Limited evidence of sustained domestique support on key climbs.
terrain_preference: Technical summit finishes (Col d'Èze signature location) and moderate-length climbs (4-8km). Struggles on extreme gradients and wet technical descents. Vulnerable in crosswinds and positioning battles on flat approaches.
vulnerability: Consistently caught out by echelon splits and positioning errors before mountain stages. Extreme gradient climbs expose limitations. Form fragility evident - injuries, COVID, and crashes derail GC campaigns repeatedly. Late-race crashes cost GC placements.
key_patterns: Attacks at 4-8km to go on final climbs across multiple Grand Tours and stage races
Repeatedly caught in crosswinds and positioning splits despite GC ambitions (Giro, Catalunya, UAE Tour)
Switches to stage-hunting mode after GC elimination, often with immediate success (Giro 2022, Tour 2019)
Struggles on extreme gradients vs. moderate climbs (Vuelta 2022 vs. Giro stage wins)
Form fragility: injuries, COVID, crashes repeatedly end GC campaigns (2022 Giro crash, Vuelta COVID, Tour crashes)
Col d'Èze signature attack location, but opponents now anticipate (2022 win, 2023 marked)
form_trajectory: neutral: 2023 Tour showed third-week resilience with stage podium and GC recovery to 4th, but earlier domestique rescue and crash vulnerability suggest inconsistent form ceiling remains unchanged from 2022 pattern
